title: Claude Helped a Hacker Find a Way to Issue Tickets to Almost Every US Music Festival
gist: Researcher Ian Carroll used Claude Opus 4.7 to bypass a web firewall and gain super-admin access to Front Gate Tickets—the Live Nation subsidiary running nearly every major US festival—exposing millions of customer records and the ability to issue unlimited free tickets, illustrating how AI makes finding real-world exploits trivially easy.

insights.claim: Claude Opus 4.7 autonomously coded a firewall-bypass technique using a 'nested SQL query' to evade detection, then wrote a script exposing samples from 500 databases of customer information—work the human researcher admits he didn't understand until reading Claude's output.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Frontier models can now independently generate novel exploit code beyond the operator's own skill—raising the offensive-security threat baseline for every web asset.
insights.quote: I had to go back and read what Claude had written to understand the bypass, because I didn't write it. Claude did it completely by itself.

insights.claim: Carroll believes Claude could plausibly have found the Front Gate exploit end-to-end with no human input at all.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Fully autonomous AI hacking of production systems is near the current capability frontier—material for cyber-insurance and defense budgeting.
insights.quote: I think there's a very good chance it could have found this exploit end-to-end without me doing anything at all
insights.locator: Even now that the flaw is fixed

insights.claim: The incident demonstrates AI can dig up hackable bugs across every facet of the internet, making the bug-finding process 'scarily easy' for both defenders and attackers.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Democratized exploit discovery pressures every organization to adopt AI-assisted auditing before adversaries do.
insights.quote: it's all held together by duct tape and prayers

insights.claim: Carroll is part of Anthropic's Cyber Verification Program, which lets approved researchers use Claude for certain hacking functions; Anthropic says that absent that approval, his use of Claude to hack Front Gate would have been detected and blocked.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Anthropic is gating offensive-security capability behind a vetted program—a governance model that may become a regulatory expectation for frontier labs.
insights.quote: if Carroll had not been part of the program, his use of Claude to hack Front Gate's systems would have been detected and blocked
insights.locator: When WIRED reached out to Anthropic

themes.theme: Live Nation / Front Gate concentration risk

insights.claim: Front Gate Tickets, a Live Nation Entertainment subsidiary, handles ticketing for practically every major US music festival except Coachella—Lollapalooza, SXSW, Austin City Limits, Bonnaroo, EDC—effectively a monopoly analogous to Ticketmaster for festivals.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Single-vendor concentration means one platform flaw exposes the entire festival ecosystem—systemic operational and reputational risk for Live Nation.
insights.quote: This is like Ticketmaster but for music festivals
insights.locator: Carroll says he first became aware of Front Gate

insights.claim: After gaining access, Carroll could reset a super administrator's password using a reset code stored in the site's own backend, and found no two-factor authentication protected admin accounts—so any leaked or guessed password grants full ticket-issuing power.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Basic security hygiene failures at a monopoly ticketing platform imply broad, cheap attack surface independent of AI.
insights.quote: No two-factor authentication prevented a leaked, stolen, or guessed password from giving someone full access.
insights.locator: Carroll was surprised to see just how easy

insights.claim: Carroll disputes Front Gate's reassurances, saying he gained super-admin privileges via a public-facing login portal with no discernible company response and that Front Gate cannot claim the vulnerability was never previously exploited.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Gap between corporate breach messaging and researcher account is a due-diligence flag on Live Nation's security posture.
insights.locator: In its response to WIRED, Front Gate's spokesperson

insights.claim: Carroll found he could add a $4,000 4-day 'Platinum' Bonnaroo ticket and issue unlimited comps for any sold-out event, exposing customer names, emails, and mailing addresses (but not credit-card data) for millions of customers and staff.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Quantifies the fraud and data-exposure blast radius—a concrete liability scenario for a Live Nation subsidiary.
insights.quote: It was pretty cool to see a ticket that's $4,000, and I could just hit a button and issue as many as I wanted
insights.locator: That was the discovery of security researcher Ian Carroll
